title: EU Data Act (Regulation (EU) 2023/2854)
official_title: Regulation (EU) 2023/2854 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 December 2023 on harmonised rules on fair access to and use of data
overview: The EU Data Act governs access to and use of data generated by connected products (IoT) and related services. Unlike GDPR (which focuses on personal data), the Data Act primarily addresses non-personal and mixed data sharing. Key provisions: users have the right to access data generated by connected products; data holders must share data with third parties upon user request; public sector bodies can access data in exceptional need; cloud provider switching is facilitated. The Data Act complements, rather than replaces, GDPR.
scope: Connected products (IoT devices — vehicles, smart appliances, industrial equipment, wearables) and related digital services. Applies to manufacturers, data holders, and data recipients in the EU.

key_provisions.chapter: Chapter II — Rights to access and use data
provisions: Users (natural or legal persons who use connected products/services) have right to access data they generate
Data holders must make data accessible to users in real time, easily, securely, free of charge
Users may share data with third parties of their choice (data portability for IoT)
Data holders may not use data about user's use of product to derive insights about user's competitors or supply chain

key_provisions.chapter: Chapter III — Obligations of data holders to make data available
provisions: Data holders must make data available to third parties upon user request
Third parties: SMEs can access data at non-discriminatory terms; large companies: fair, reasonable terms
Data holder may impose reasonable compensation for making data available
Data can only be used for the specified purpose agreed with the user

key_provisions.chapter: Chapter V — Public sector access in exceptional need
provisions: Public sector bodies and EU institutions can request data from private entities in exceptional need (e.g., emergency, public interest)
Stricter conditions than Chapter III: cannot use for commercial purposes, must delete after use
Compensation capped at technical and organisational costs
Cloud providers must facilitate switching to another provider (removal of switching costs, data portability)
Switching charges to be phased out by September 2027
Functional equivalence: providers must ensure customer can continue processing without interruption
Restrictions on transfer of non-personal data to third countries where transfer would conflict with EU law
Framework for data spaces and data altruism

relationship_to_gdpr: When personal data is involved in IoT data, GDPR applies. The Data Act addresses the non-personal data or mixed data that GDPR does not fully govern. Data Act Article 1(5): 'This Regulation does not affect the application of Union law on the protection of personal data.'

penalty_structure.member_state_rules: Member states must lay down rules on penalties. Must be effective, proportionate, dissuasive.
penalty_structure.typical_maximum: Most member states implementing fines up to 1–4% of annual global turnover or fixed amounts.
penalty_structure.cloud_switching: Specific penalties for cloud providers failing to facilitate switching.

cross_references.relevance: GDPR applies when IoT data includes personal data. Data Act addresses non-personal data. Both frameworks apply simultaneously for mixed data sets. GDPR rights (access, portability, erasure) apply to personal data components.
AI systems using IoT data must comply with Data Act (data access rights) and AI Act (risk classification, obligations). High-risk AI systems in connected products must meet both.

Data Governance Act establishes data intermediaries and data altruism organisations. Data Act complements by establishing rules for private data sharing. Together they form the European Data Strategy.
